cited_text: Every company says they do that. Most of them are full of shit. What they've got are people around them who are telling them what the leader wants to hear. And the leader thinks because they're hearing what they want to hear, that he got this really insightful group.
response_text:
The setup for everything else Catmull says about the Braintrust.
Leaders surround themselves with people who reflect back
what they want to hear — and mistake that for **insight**.

cited_text: I would say that a lot of people like to get on one layer. You get on the one layer and you form a decision. Can't do that. You got to keep peeling away the layers to figure out what's really underneath it. And it's a shortcut. To make a decision quickly based upon a little bit of information. And getting at underlying factors is inherently a long term strategy and it's a difficult one and it's timely.
response_text:
The honest admission most management advice skips.
Fast decisions feel efficient — they're usually just
**shallow**. Peeling layers costs time you often don't want to spend.

cited_text: Steve had such a powerful voice that it didn't matter when he spoke. He was going to have this extremely strong effect on the dynamics of the room, and he understood that. So it was the reason he didn't come. I said, basically, the dynamics don't work if you're in the room and that we need you for this. And he understood that.
response_text:
The reason Jobs was banned wasn't that he was wrong —
it's that his presence **warped** the room regardless of content.
Catmull understood that dynamics ARE the product.

cited_text: However, I'm in every one of these meetings, there was nothing that Steve ever said that had not been said by somebody else before in one of those meetings. Now, what's the difference what's happening there?
response_text:
This is the sharpest observation in the excerpt.
The Braintrust was already surfacing the right answers —
Jobs just had the **authority weight** to make directors actually hear them.

cited_text: This is so important, to get all this working right, that this is what our product's based upon, is this group of people working well together. So paying attention to the dynamics of those rooms is the job. They're the ones that are making the movies. I'm not making the movie. I'm just trying to make sure they work well together.
response_text:
Catmull's clearest statement of what a leader actually does.
Not output — **conditions**. Rare for someone at his level
to say it this plainly and mean it.

cited_text:
And the logic that he gave at the time was that because we knew this was not a boutique film, this is going to be a big deal. After this film, we had to deliver two more films. But at the end of delivering the next two films, because now we had an experience that nobody else did and we could get those other two out with all the experience behind us that when we're done that we will have created Michael Eisner's Worst Nightmare.

**Speaker A** [0:24:30]: A competitor which, funded by, in part by Disney, funded their own competitor.
response_text:
Jobs' logic for the simultaneous IPO and Toy Story release.
Complete the contract, gain the experience, go public,
then **renegotiate from strength** — or become a direct competitor.
Chess while Eisner was playing checkers.

cited_text: But nobody ever asked what we're doing or what's different. Why is it that Pixar is so different? It's very curious. So now for me, it's just observations like, okay, why aren't they curious? Why aren't they asking? Then we signed the contract, the PI Picture contract, to continue it, which continues that right to see everything that we're doing now. We're very successful and they're very unsuccessful. And still nobody ever asked, what are you doing? And there are no secrets. We're not hiding anything ever.
response_text: Disney had contractual access to everything Pixar was doing, yet nobody asked why Pixar was succeeding while Disney was declining.

cited_text: And for me the basis was what's the spirit of the team? Because we all know it doesn't work. But if they're really working together and they're laughing and then they're angsting and all that together, then that's when you say, okay, we just keep going. We just keep trying to solve it.
response_text: Catmull's real-time signal for whether to keep pushing a struggling film was the **spirit of the team** — not the quality of the current cut.

cited_text: If you take on hard things, then you need to spend more time trying to figure out, you're trying to figure out how to be different in what you're doing. So if you take on a hard problem and you just keep pushing on it, then the fact that it was hard is what's going to make it different.
response_text: Difficulty isn't a warning sign — it's the mechanism. Easy problems produce derivative work; hard problems force genuine novelty.

cited_text: But in terms of quality as the business best business plan, it's, you know, this is related to this, this great book, the man who Broke Capitalism about Jack Welch.
response_text: Catmull contrasts this with Jack Welch — Forbes' Manager of the Century — whose short-term growth obsession gutted GE's research, fragmented the company, and exported the same destructive principles to Boeing.

cited_text: I think in the year 2000 is when it was Forbes magazine named him as the CEO or manager of the century. Utter disaster. response_text: Catmull contrasts this with Jack Welch — Forbes' Manager of the Century — whose short-term growth obsession gutted GE's research, fragmented the company, and exported the same destructive principles to Boeing.

cited_text: I do think that if I know that I'm wrong half the time that I catch it earlier, I spend less time on the wrong decision. So it looks like the ratio is different, but the reality is I'm just wrong half the time. I think it's important to think that way. And that allows you to say, okay, maybe this is one of those times. I would Rather catch it sooner rather than later.
response_text: Catmull's actual edge wasn't a better hit rate — it was a faster correction rate. Holding the prior that you're probably wrong makes you notice contradicting evidence sooner.

cited_text: But the important thing that I came away with was that asking the question might have been a natural thing to ask, but trying to answer it is an act of separation. That's the problem with it because I'm trying to say, how am I separate from others? And really, I never was.
response_text:
Catmull's most important insight in the clip.
The instinct to isolate your personal contribution
is itself the problem — it breaks the mental model
that made the work possible in the first place.

cited_text: And so what he's saying, well, how much can I do on my own? It's not the right question. It's how much can I do with others?
response_text:
Clean reframe worth keeping.
The guy who left to "test himself without a
safety net" misunderstood what the net was —
it was just good colleagues thinking together.

cited_text: But if you don't have a film out there, then you want people to enjoy going to the movies. That is, people that you might call your competitors are producing things that other people enjoy. You have a vested interest in wanting the rest of the industry to be healthy.
response_text:
Lucas's competitive logic is structurally
different to most industries. A healthy ecosystem
lifts all boats — your rivals making great work
grows the whole pie, not shrinks yours.

cited_text: I say that because there were three times in the history of Pixar in which a group arose that felt like they were second class and we missed it and we were looking for it. So this is really hard and it's very subtle.
response_text:
This is the uncomfortable admission.
They were actively scanning for this failure mode
and still got caught three times. Most orgs
aren't even scanning.

cited_text: So regardless of what you say, they don't want to look like they're out of place with these people they respect because they're very successful. It doesn't matter what you say, they're emulating the behaviors that they see. And what they see are people who are going home at night.
response_text:
Culture is **observed behaviour**, not stated values.
New hires watched senior people go home at 6pm
and copied it — while those same seniors
complained the new cohort had no energy.
Neither group said a word.

cited_text: If somebody violates a rule of common sense, people do once in a while you call them on the, the carpet, you tell them you can't do it, but you don't make a rule so that nobody else makes the same mistake. Because as soon as you make more rules, then people are worried about, what are all these rules? Who do I have to ask for permission? So what we wanted was that people would do things without feeling they need to ask permission for everything.
response_text:
Every new rule is a **permission tax** on everyone
who wasn't the problem. Catmull's instinct is right
and almost universally ignored by scaling orgs.
